One is how you use tonality to essentially control your prospects internal monologue, meaning what they're saying to themselves about you, about what you're saying, how they perceive what you're saying. For instance, if I say, hi, my name is Jordan Belfort on a sales call, if that's my time, my name is Jordan Belfort, that's my tonality. So to down, hi, I'm Jordan Belfort, right? And that, so, no, but now they will be saying that to you. They'll say, yes, uh-huh, like they're thinking that and saying something else to you. So just remember that what your prospect is thinking is not going to be the same thing that their expression tells you or their words do. So they might be saying, yes, yes, uh-huh, and then they'll say, fuck, another sales call. First I say, hey, it's Jordan Belfort calling, and I change my number, I should give it to say, hey, Jordan Belfort calling. For instance, hey, Jordan Belfort calling, and they'll like, ooh, now by using an uptoe there, I phrase a declarer saying, hi, Jordan Belfort calling, as a, hey, Jordan Belfort calling, and now all of a sudden it flips their dialogue, their internal monologue, and they're like, who was that? Do I know Jordan Belfort? Okay, an idea. So I stop them from narrating, self-saturating in a way that defeats the purpose of the sale, to get into reporting them, to get my message for us. So right from the first word out of my mouth, you use tonality to actually take control of your prospect's internal monologue and then move it in a direction that serves you and also serves the prospect. So it's not like you're trying to manipulate it in a bad way, you're trying to serve them because you're going to be selling them something, offering them something that they need, that they want, that's going to help them. You get that? So tonality allows, it essentially opens up the door for you. It opens up the door for you, it allows people to have it. So that's your higher level of tonality, which is that using tonality as you're going to teach you all ten tonalities today, right? So that's using all these tonalities in rapid fire of succession and at the end of the word it sums up to. So the overarching principle that drives everything else to the straight line is the fact that you have literally four seconds to establish three crucial things, or else you basically can't influence anyone, and I'll tell you exactly what they are. In those first two seconds, if you are not perceived as being number one, sharp as a tap, number two, enthusiastic as hell, enthusiastic as hell, number three, an expert in your field. The second aspect of charisma is called having appropriate body language, appropriate body language. Let me tell you something, from the moment someone lays eyes on you in person, they are processing your body language, what you're wearing, how you move, how close you stand to them, what type of eye contact you think, how you shake hands, how you move your body water, how you move your body water, listening, all those little breaths, how you lean forward, how you lean back. The human brain is incredibly in depth with this, it processes it, and in order to be charismatic, it's about having appropriate body language, knowing how to move your body when you're talking and water listening. From what you wear to the eye contact, how you shake hands, right? So number one, it's about affecting tonality, and number two, it's about having appropriate body language. The third pillar of having awesome charisma, massive charisma, is not saying stupid shit, which for many people can be the toughest part of all. You never might be in the heart and the mind of your prospect on purpose. And once you do this, once you start using these tonalities on purpose, you will be shocked at how easy influence becomes by the wine. So we're going to start with the first one. Let's go to number one first. I'm just going to do this. The first tonality is the tonality of certainty. So this, look now, here's what I'm going to do, and I'm going to explain what the tonality is, and I'm going to give you examples of good tonality and bad tonality, and I'm going to ask you which one sounds better to you. It'll be plainly obvious. And then again, we'll revisit this in a month or so from now. We have to be this around and we have to interact. So nobody wants to be certain. We explained to you what certainty is. The tonality of certainty is what you're so sure about something. You get that point, of course, to your prospect. Now remember that at the highest level, sales is the transference of emotion. That's what you're doing. And the emotion that you're transferring is the emotion of certainty. The certainty about your product is awesome. The certainty about you, the salesperson, the awesome that you want to deal with you, get your brain, get your best of what you do. The certainty about the COVID that stands behind the product. Certainty, certainty, three tens, right? We have three elements of certainty. And the tonality of certainty doesn't sound like this. Well, J.M., let me tell you, this is the best product I have right now. This thing is going to solve all your needs. It's going to remove all your pain. And it is by far the best deal in the marketplace. I repeat that. J.M., this is the best product out there. It's going to resolve all your needs. It's going to remove all your pain. And it is by far the best product, the best deal in the marketplace. That's the words that would imply certainty without the right tonality. Now, let me say to you again, J.M., just please, this is the best product in the marketplace. It's going to resolve all your needs, remove all your pain, and there is nothing close to it in the marketplace. Now, you can tell me which one of those sounds better to you. The second one, right? J.M., this is the best product. J.M., this is the best product. Now, notice that when I employ certainty, I'm not yelling, what's, it's like, J.M., this is a better, I'm like, I am the fucking of that Pizzel, the holy little, you'd be on the influential, ah, why am I Pizzel on infrovertial? That's not certainty. That's like a fucking circus attraction. And maybe it works like infrovertials on TV, that's just blast shit in your ear, right? But in the world of influence and persuasion and selling, that is not the way it works, you know? Yell and scream to create certainty. Certain people can be a whisper, but it's a power, and it just, you say, this is so, and it's intensity, you're speaking with staccato-like beats. Boom, boom, boom. It's not passive communication. So that's your first tonality. It's a tonality of absolute, it's a tonality of absolute certainty. Absolute certainty. Okay, now, absolute, it's absolute community of what we're doing with voice-making. It's a volcano, a seabing volcano. I'm trying all these different ways to explain to you, just give you one, which will bubble, hit you, and you'll manifest that yourself, okay? So it's absolute certainty, that's number one, okay? Number two, second tonality. We're going to please look at, it's going to be sincerity or utter sincerity. Now, utter sincerity is not one certainty. You're not going to be, ah, ah, ah, alright, it's, it's sincerity is calm. It's a velvety soft tone where you're talking from the heart. And the words, so now watch. The words that, when I talk about certainty, but I'm using a tonality of certainty, the words that I want my prospect to be hearing are, damn, does he believe this? Wow, he is really, so my, there's their hearing, I say, this is something like, wow, he really needs it. That's what they're thinking as you speak. When I use the sincerity of my John, let me say this. What they're saying, wow, he's being really honest here. He's being honest, he's being forthright. He really is a sincere person. He means what he says. There's no pressure involved here. That's the point I'm getting across with the tonality of sincerity. So I would say, watch, John, you're giving me one shot, and believe me. So that first thought was, certainty and believe me, the only problem you'll have, silky smooth, the only problem you'll have is that you can only get six months ago, and you get, you start it then. That's sincerity. Silky smooth, the only problem you'll have is that you can only get six months ago, and you get, you start it then. Silky smooth, no pressure, heart to heart, friend to friend. I'm speaking since these are the, are the emotions that you're looking to get across when you use the tonality of sincerity. So if you say so, and John, John, John, the only problem you'll have is you need to call me six months ago. What the fuck? They'll be like, I don't trust the person, John, the only problem you'll have is you need to call me six months ago and get started then. Now that's the third tone out which is called, you are, click that please, is called your reasonable man. Okay? And the reasonable man tone is a tone out that implies that what you're saying is totally reasonable. It's like, you know, anyone would do this. You say, John, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't get started six months ago because you'd already be making money. And now here comes the reasonable man tone. Sound fair enough? Sound good? I'm reasonable, your reasonable, those are the applying words that your prospect will hear when you use the reasonable man tone. You get it? In other words, they're hearing your voice, you say, John, you're giving one shot that certainty and believing the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. That's sincerity. And then the third tone is sound fair enough to get thrown sound fair. I'm reasonable, you're like, if you have 60 seconds, I'd like to share an identity. You got it? You got it? I'm reasonable, you're reasonable. So you have to say, imagine if you said, hey John, you got 60 minutes, we're both reasonable people, I'm sure you have 60 seconds, right John? You can't say the words, but the tonality gets your point across. It's the extra words that your prospect hears. So you'd say, so now we'll be watching, we're gonna link these three together, both with tonalities, to show you how you'd close the sale in the tonalities we used to close. When I go through my whole picture, I would ask this, I would ask into the order, I'd say, and John, believe me, the only problem you'll have is that you didn't buy more. Sound fair enough? And then you shut up. Those are your first three tonalities. You use these tonalities every time you close, you're gonna use those three, using many other places as well. One of them, before please. Next time, use your toes to do that one. The next tonality is the tonality of scarcity. Urgency, they go together. Scarcity is the basic human notion, basic human sense, that is how we operate. Is that we always want more of what we perceive there to be less of. When something is in short supply, we're about to run out, we want to get it more, because we're scared of missing out. And this goes back to the caveman days when food was in short supply and junk food was in short supply. Nowadays we refer to it as success, he's got a scarcity mentality. She's got a scarcity, they're not positive people, they're thinking from the short, there's less of stuff versus more. And that's good, it comes to the overarching principles of success. But when it comes to influence and persuasion, scarcity is a very, very powerful tool. Now there are three types of scarcity. Now break it down for you. The first one is called verbal scarcity. And verbal scarcity is simply saying words that imply scarcity. Let's say we're talking about a BMW, a car, you're selling cars. You say, oh there's only one of these BMWs left with black on black interior. So if you don't get it now, you need to be six points away from one another. Those are the words that imply something is in short supply. There's only six of these lobes of red left. We're not voting for another month after that. So that's just basic word that implies scarcity. Now let's kick it up a notch by applying the tonality of scarcity on top of the words. So you could say, oh there's only, this is the last love, black on black BMW, 750iL, and we're not going to work it on the next year. So that's basically the basic word that we're talking about, and we're not going to work it on the next year. Or you could say, yeah Jim, this is the only black on black BMW and we're not going to work it on the next year. Which one sounds better? Which one hits you in the gut saying, shit, if I want this now, I'm not going to just get a buy because of that, but if they were thinking about it, this is why they're at nap. So we often do scarcity to create urgency. And so it's not enough that you get to look and buy. You want to buy now. And that's the power of scarcity. So again, you have verbal scarcity which is your words, something that is in short supply. Second, you have your tonal scarcity which says, okay it's in short supply and my tonality lets you know how short that supply is and it's running out. It's not a whisper of power that's going to come. Third type of scarcity is called informational scarcity. Meaning, even the information itself is in short supply. For instance, say, there's only one BMW black on black, we're not ordering one till the next month and no one even knows there's only one left. Next week we're going to actually make an announcement. There's only one to go. How long was it going to last then? In other words, even the fact that it's in short supply, the information itself is in short supply. Those three together they are very powerful in propelling a prospect to take action. So we want to use all three whenever possible. Okay? That's your tonality of scarcity. What's the next one? The fun? Money aside. Okay? So your money aside tonality, I also call your unfulfetting and unhygienic. Now I think one of the first topics I covered with these live traders was the process of looping. Looping is the secret to us to the back half of the straight line system. Meaning, it's what we use to take the common objections that typically come off at the end of the sale, right? After you've made a presentation. And how we take those common objections and use them as opportunities to create more certainty for each of the three pens and then we seamlessly transition into a close without breaking report. That's looping, right? Now, the way we begin our first loop is using this hypothetical or money aside for now, but explain. I ask for the order from the first slide, right? Blini, give me one chance. Sound fair enough? And the prospect says, that sounds pretty good, but let me think about it. Very common response. Well, yeah, sounds good. Let me call you back. Let me ask you about wipers. So the way we handle any initial objection is we use a straight line process called deflection. We use a deflection. Deflection means we do not add to that initial objection head on. We deflect it instead and we transition to a more productive topic, mainly our first ten, meaning the prospect. What you want to know is does the prospect, how high are they on the certainty scale in terms of our product? So here's the words. Let me say what words. Jim, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? I repeat, those are the words. Jim, I hear what you're saying, but let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like it again? Those are about as toneless as I can do. I have really good tone-ups. It's fun if you've almost had no tone-ups. That's pretty talk-ready. I want you to listen to me again, and now I'm going to say it through the long way. So imagine I just drew my presentation. I've asked you for the order from the first time, and your response was, let me think about it. And I say to you, well Jim, I hear what you're saying. Well, here's your question. You bat, you get someone completely off-filter, you break the floor, they no longer need to be talking to you anymore. That's the war, that's an aggressive tone-out. It was an angry tone-out. You raced with some certainty. Versus this tone-out. And I want you to listen very closely. And I want you to listen to your own monologue. Tell me what words you're thinking as I say this. The way tonality works in terms of clarity is that when you use a certain tonality, it causes the prospect to hear additional words on top of the words that you actually said. So what you do is you put their conscious mind essentially in the overdrive and it's trying to keep up with all these additional words and gestures, and it means that you layer it on top of your words, your words are your words, right? And they say, yeah, it makes sense, and I say, exactly, not exactly, enter their world where they are, so what you're probably doing right now is you're coming on a bit too strong, I want you to slow it down, start with more, less certainty, more sincerity, and then you pace them, and then you pace them, and then slowly become more certain, and you'll see a huge difference, okay? How can I win using straight line if the product I sell is famous like beer, Coca-Cola, Pepsi? We're in the end the only thing that matters is price. Yeah, well, that's a good point. So listen, at the end of the day, what you're talking about is a commodity. So yeah, when you hit to certain things where it's just very, very price oriented, but remember this, it's typically going to be one, more than one person trying to sell that person at this price at the same price. So while it might not be about prices, it also may be about customer service, it's a tangible relation. All things being equal. At the end of the day, people buy from people who they like and who they trust and who they know will stand behind them and there's problems and so forth. No, so it's all these other... So yes, price is going to be your basic thing, right? It costs, right? You can't give it 30% higher than everybody else would expect to get the sale, right? But if you come at the same price as everyone else, well, maybe 2%, there's all these other intangibles that come into play. They focus on those. But you're right. And commodity and price is price. Next. Where the deals that start via text translate tone to text? Listen, I take tremendous care when I write my emails to make sure that I get my tonality across in my emails by putting things in italicized, bulging them, making sure my sentences are short. It gets a little bit harder when you're texting because, you know, text by nature are supposed to be shorter. So it's a bit more challenging. But remember, you know, you can't italicize and bulge those texts in platforms, right? So I would keep my text short if I was trying to use a text word to drive me through another platform. It's very different to close someone just by text. You can get them to another platform just by text and then close them there. That's the truth. All right. Last question. How do I fight against gatekeepers? Well, you know, again, the basic elements of gatekeepers are that you can't get through every gatekeeper. But the general rules of gatekeepers are that number one, the less information is better. You don't say, hi, I'm Jordan Belvoor, a quantum XYZ company in the automotive industry. Is Mr. Smith there? Yeah, very little chance of getting Mr. Smith like that. So I'd say, hey, it's a, hi, it's Jordan. Is Mr. Smith there? So I'm gonna say that either. I'd say, hey, is Jordan, is John there?
